findings.description: SQL injection: the email argument is interpolated directly into the query via an f-string instead of a parameterized placeholder, allowing authentication bypass and data exfiltration.

Inverted expiry check: 'if session.expires_at > utcnow()' deletes and rejects sessions that are still valid while falling through to accept sessions whose expires_at is in the past, so expired tokens authenticate and fresh ones do not.
Consequence of the inverted comparison on line 105: expired session tokens are treated as live and return a user id, defeating session timeout entirely.

LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but never referenced; lockout after MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS is permanent (only cleared by a successful login) rather than time-windowed, so a legitimate user is locked out indefinitely.

find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users' and filters in Python, performing a full-table load on every lookup despite the docstring promising a single record; this scales linearly with the user table.
Sessions are stored only in an in-memory dict, so they are lost on process restart and not shared across workers/processes, and grow unbounded unless cleanup_expired is called on a schedule.

Login uses early returns (unknown email -> immediate return, locked -> return before hashing, bad password -> hash then return), creating a timing/response side channel that enables user enumeration and lockout-state probing.

When the account is already locked the failed_attempts counter is not incremented and no audit beyond a warning is recorded, so repeated attempts against a locked account are silently ignored rather than tracked.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated as of Python 3.12 and returns a naive datetime; mixing naive UTC timestamps risks comparison/serialization bugs and should be datetime.now(timezone.utc).
The log message 'session expired' is emitted from the branch that actually fires for still-valid sessions (due to the line 105 inversion), so logs misreport why the session was dropped.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 at 100,000 iterations is below current OWASP guidance (~600,000 for SHA-256), weakening resistance to offline brute force.
find_user_by_email loads full User records including password_hash and salt for every row into memory just to match an email, widening exposure of credential material beyond what the lookup needs.
Reliance on 'SELECT *' couples row construction to physical column order/presence; an explicit column list would be more robust to schema changes.
No type/None guards on password or email inputs (e.g., password.encode() will raise on non-str), so malformed callers get an unhandled exception rather than a clean auth failure.
cleanup_expired must be invoked manually and there is no scheduler or lazy purge on access, so the only practical eviction path for the in-memory store is dead given the broken validate_session.
